sentences.text: Yes.
He was quite successful at this, I don't have to say.
The superficial elements, the, the nobodies, the no goods immediately joined the party.
And they, they cherished their uniforms, their-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the black ties and, and the green shirts, and, and the bands.
And they were roaming the streets sometimes.
His-- this teacher was actually my fencing teacher, given at early age.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] his son was very little, maybe five years old.
He, he would walk on the street with a whip.
And he knew who we were.
And everybody knew who we were.
Everybody knew who was Jewish, who wasn't Jewish.
And he would meet me.
And he would raise his hand-- the five-year-old child.
And he'd whip me with the whip.
He-- and he would-- he could hardly say Jew-- Jid.
He would say, 'Jid, Jid, Jid.' What can you do with a child like this, you know, where he came from?
So finally, when I went to high school, this Koppanyi was our gym teacher.
And there was an anecdote that I have to tell you, because it's still in my mind.
And I see the picture.
There
Was an Olympic fencer, a Jewish man, whose name was John Schporer, a tall, lean man, a wonderful person, who was my English teacher when I was nine years old.
My parents hired him.
And this Nazi, Koppanyi, he liked to fence very much.
And he would invite Schporer to fence with him, in spite of he being a Jew and this guy was a-- the head of the Nazi party.
And we would sit down in the gymnasium.
And we were watching these two men fence. the-- with sword.
They had their white outfits and the mask.
And my gym teacher had a, a watch.
And the watch band that was hanging down from his pocket was made out of beads, like the Indians make the beads.
And these beads made a swastika.
So it was a, a green and white strip of beads with the swastika on it.
So these people would fence.
And like Cyrano, each time when he scored-- my friend, John Schporer-- he also hit that band that the watch which was hanging down from his pocket with his sword.
So they were fencing.
He always won, of course.
He was with marvelous fencer.
And each time he hit this, some of those beads would, would fall and break.
And they would roll all over the gymnasium.
And when they finished fencing, that was gone.
And I still this picture-- it's those little beads are running all over.
I said to myself, thumb up for you, Schporer.
Just to finish this, what happened to my gym teacher?
He was shot by the Russians.
He shot in the head in-- when they tried to stop the Russians of coming in.
And what happened to Schporer?
He became the, the president of the fencing society in Hungary.
And, and he was a, a prominent guy in the film industry in Hungary also.
I think he's still alive, 87-some years old.
The war, of course, broke out in 1939.
But I gather from what you're saying that the Nazi movement really got going in your town at least, and probably in other--
Yes.
Well, when Hungary got into the war, I think in, in the '40s, early '40s, maybe '41, people were taken.
Jews were taken to labor camps and, and forced labor in, in the Soviet Union.
And the Hungarian army participated in the war.
The, the Nazi movement was very, very strong all over the country, very well-supported.

And again, as a child, I remember when the head of the Nazi party nationwide-- his name was Szalasi, S-Z-A-L-A-S-I-- who became the president of the country eventually during the Nazis, he came to my town.
And he was not allowed to speak publicly, because Governor Horthy banned him because he, he was in competition with the government.
He was in uniform.
He had a pockmarked face.
And he stood in the middle of the marketplace on a podium.
There were thousands of people around nit.
And he hailed.
And all he said, what kind of country do we want?
And the crowd roared, Jew-free country, Jew-free country.
And then he got off the podium.
And he came around.
I was standing there.
And he was shaking hands with everybody.
And I stepped back.
Oh, god, I don't want to shake the hand of this man.
What happened to me?
I'm here.
What happened to him?
The Americans captured him in Austria after the war, Szalasi.
And he was hung.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was mainly a, a farmland where we lived.
It's a plain farmers' country.
And the farmers were not too inclined to, to participate in any Nazi movement.
It, it was the-- it was the very poor-- the ones who eventually became communists after the war, the same people.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all in all, I, I don't have many bad words for my village.
Mostly decent people lived there, except for this few, who-- who came to the surface.
During the early part of the war, what was life-- what life like for the-- well, for, for the Jewish population?
I'm thinking, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let's say, in the years from '39 to 1940, 1941, that period.
Well, the, the government, the cabinet, the administration was anti-Semitic in Hungary.
Jewish laws were created-- the first Jewish law, the second Jewish law.
And each one brought more and more restrictions.
Just to give you an example, there was a law that Jews couldn't bear guns.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father sold guns and shotguns and so on and so forth.
They would take it away.
Then was another law that Jews couldn't sell wine.
So my father couldn't sell any more wine.
So there was a gradual decrease in your livelihood.
And a gradual restriction in your lifestyle and, and, and lives through these laws.
Jews couldn't have a public service.
They couldn't be employed by public.
And then came the worst, of course, it was the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the so-called clean race law, that a Jewish man, a Jewish person, could not have sexual relation with a Christian.
And this was terribly serially punishable if it happened, it occurred, or if they charged somebody with this.
So these laws centrally came from Budapest in a country which, at one time, was extremely liberal.
And my father always mentioned the good old times during Kaiser Franz Joseph and when the monarchy was quite liberal.
And the Jews flourished at that time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What happened exactly that Horthy went along on the-- the governor of the country-- he went along with all these laws, which were brought by, by his anti-Semitic cabinet.
And eventually, he was himself deposed.
And, and the government was taken over by the Nazis.
And he was under house arrest himself.
So there was a gradual shifting and shifting to the right and more and more to radicalizing the country toward Nazism.
So when the Germans came in, not just there was no resistance, but there was an absolute cooperation in, in any way and anywhere by the police, by the gendarmerie, by the government.
The Germans came in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] March or April, April of 1944.
I was less than 14.
I do.
Yes, I recall very, very clearly.
We knew that the Germans arrived to Hungary.
And they, they took over in a friendly manner with no resistance, as I say.
And on that day in the morning, somebody said the Germans came to town.
So we all went out then to see on the Main Street.
They came with their motorcycles and the gondola and their black leather jackets and raincoats and, and helmets, just like we see today in the Second World War movies.
Everything was black and fearsome.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the, the captain, he got up with the motorcycle.
And he started to walk on Main Street.
And we were watching him.
He was hitting his boots with his riding stick and then pointed to my friend's house, which was a villa on Main Street.
And his mother was my pediatrician.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said this is going to be German headquarters.
And they had to move out immediately from that house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from then on, the events rolled very rapidly after the Germans had arrived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jews knew-- we, we all knew that something is coming, that it will be terrible.
And we didn't know what exactly and how this will happen.
And one day, the Gestapo took the prominent Jews away.
Some of the men were already in the Soviet, in, in working camps.
But some of them were still at home.
And they were taken.
My father was not taken.
And we saw on the street when they were taken.
We were working.
We were kind of forced labor with children.
And the significant event was this-- is that we had to wear a yellow star.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that, that was really, a really a big blow.
As I told you before that we were always-- felt different.
We, we were treated differently.
But that was really the absolute mark that we were different.
And we will be treated differently.
We had to sew the yellow star on our left side over our hearts, told us exactly where to put it, how to put it.
And if you didn't put it on and it would catch you, it would be severely punished.
So that was a period of yellow star.
And we went to school with this.
And being in a high school full of anti-Semitic kids with the yellow star, it's not imaginable what, what children would suffer.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Then we would go sometimes to the swimming pool.
We could, could go.
And it we be dunked and, and beaten in the water by big kids who, who wore swastikas on their swimming trunk.
And I was raging mad.
But I couldn't do anything.
I was just a little boy.
And So one day after a beating like this, I ran through the cabin where we were dressing.
And I saw a boy there who was a little bigger than me.
And I went there.
And I didn't ask a question.
I just beat him up.
And then he started to cry.
And he pulled down his wet bathing tank to get dressed, and I saw that he was circumcised.
So I was astounded.
And I asked him, are you Jewish?
He said, yes.
So I went there and helped him and kissed him and took him home on my bicycle.
And we became very good friends.
And I have the opportunity to show his picture.
And his name was Georgie Herman.
And he perished in Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Childhood in those years was very difficult, extremely difficult.
And you asked me before if I had asked myself about my own Judaism.
I really had a lot of questions at that time.
Why?
And why, why are we treated like this, when my father was the most decent person I ever met?
He never cheated anyone.
He never lied.
He was an angel.
Why are we treated like this?
Tell it to a child.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
The rapid sequence brought about getting to go to the ghetto.
One day, the order came that you have to leave your homes immediately with a limited amount of material that you could take with you, possessions.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They designated a, a whole block of homes where Jews used to live before.
And they moved all the Jews into the area that they called a ghetto. that was the first time I, I, I encountered this expression, ghetto.
I never even knew what it was before.
They boarded the windows.
They put several families in one apartment.
And the food was very limited.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Fortunately, the people we were together were all nice and good.
And, and our being together was harmonious, in spite of the difficulties.
And this is the time for me to, to mention charitable, good Christians who came at night and knocked on our boarded window.
They said, open the window.
We brought you some food.
And they brought milk and bread and, and some fruit.
And they were really risking their life or, or freedom, because the gendarmerie was outside.
And it was-- by death penalty-- was forbidden for Christians to, to help out Jews.
That gave us some consolation that there were good people among the bad.
And there were good seeds.
And, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and we spent some time in, in this ghetto.
Many of the fathers had been taken to, to camps, ordered by the Gestapo.
So there were mainly women and children and some men, like my father.
One day, with our-- on the courtyard.
And we hear humming of planes very high up in the air.
And we looked outside.
As a, a child, I was absolutely fascinated by this, of hundreds and hundreds of little, silvery, shiny planes in formation were flying east.
As I learned later, they were flying Liberators, or B-17s.
They were flying to Ploiesti, to Romania, to bomb the oil fields, hundreds and hundreds of them.
And we knew they were Americans.
And if I could describe this to you, what, what it feels to a child [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] to look up and see friends.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't want to get emotional, but it can happen.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] One day, the captain of the gendarmerie who was also a mensch told us that I'm afraid that soon, you're going to be taken away from here.
He was very good to us, another good man.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father told him, we will give you a jewelry or something.
He said, I don't want anything from you.
Just when you come back, you remember me.
And it was a uniformed man and good person.
So that one day really came when they--
I don't remember his name, unfortunately.
I remember that the war, my parents and my, and my friend's father, who was a lawyer, tried to get this man out of a communist concentration camp.
Because they were all put in automatically, because they were in the gendarmerie.
And they could not get him out.
It was just impossible.
Yes.
Because they would first say, you can't sell this.
And then they'd say, you can't sell that.
That's correct, yes.
Well, when we went to the ghetto, they said, you close your business and leave it there.
And that's it.
They took it away.
They took away with all what was in inside.
No.
No.
No.
No compensation.
No.
None.
Warned us that we're going to be--
Yeah, right.
A couple of days later, they came-- the gendarmerie-- said, you all get up, pack up even less what you had before, just a rucksack, a, a bag and a blanket.
And we'll take you.
So they took all the Jews who were there in the ghetto, confined.
They took us to a storage area for wheat.
And they kept us for another day to wait to be deported.
That's what happened.
And while we were waiting, obviously, the village learned about it.
And one of my English teacher, whose name was Breznanski-- he was also a fine gentleman, a good soul-- appeared in, in that storage area with suitcases that he opened up.
And hundreds and hundreds of prepared sandwiches were in there, which he gave us.
And he distributed to the people in front of the police and gendarmerie without any fear and wished us good luck.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we waited-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we waited, too afraid-- for afraid to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to take over us.
Anyway, they took us to the railroad station.
And that was the first time I encountered the new terminology, the boxcar or cattle car, that came into our lives.
So they put us in cattle cars in that little town.
And a, a good number of people were out witnessing this and cheering and hollering, they're taking the Jews away.
They're taking the Jews.
The Jews are leaving town.
And here again, before I, I give up on all mankind and, and, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and try to remember something pleasant, a taxi arrived to the railroad station.
And a Presbyterian-- or I say reformed Presbyterian minister came with his wife and baby.
They got out of the car.
They came to the boxcar where my family was with the open door.
He came quite close.
He lifted his baby, put it in the boxcar.
And he said, kiss my baby before you leave.
And that's again in front of the village, in front of the Nazis, in front of the gendarmerie, fearlessly.
I who lived through those times afterwards and communism, I can appreciate now far more than then.
But it gave us really courage to go in and face the life ahead.
Prior to the occupation, yes, we were still in high school, thank to the principal of the high school, whose name was Miklos Nagy-- N-A-G-Y, like Imre Nagy.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Hungary, the high school is eight years.
Its called a gymnasium.
And it was extremely important.
That's the only high school we had in Szeghalom.
They did not want to take any Jewish children to high school.
There would be no possibility.
There was nothing around the town anywhere in the area.
This was the only high school.
And the principal insisted that every Jewish child who was capable, he should be admitted to school and goes through with a high school education.
And he said if that wouldn't happen, he would resign.
The man was so important and, and so good.
And he was the friend of the minister who became the president of Hungary later that he, he really put his stamp in the school.
And all the teachers followed his, his rules and his behavior, except for my phys ed teacher that I mentioned before.
This-- incidently, this high school principal became the Secretary for Education after the war in Hungary.
